title: Quick check: the standard EDR incident workflow

questions.prompt: Which set names the six steps of the standard EDR incident workflow, in order?
choices: Claim, review, approve remediations, verify, close, document.

choices.label: Investigate, classify, recommend, approve, verify, document.
choices.rationale: Investigate / classify / recommend are the SOC's pre-handoff steps, not the tech's workflow.

choices.label: Read, decide, act, document, close, file.
choices.rationale: Skips claim and verify.

choices.label: Claim, approve remediations, close, document.
choices.rationale: Skips review and verify. Verify is the step new techs skip first when the queue is busy.

questions.explanation: The six-step spine. Each step has its own habit; verify and document are the two new techs most often skip.

questions.prompt: What does the verify step actually mean?

choices.label: Re-read the Incident Report to make sure you understood the Recommendation.
choices.rationale: Verification is the endpoint-state check, not a re-read of the report. They're different checks.

choices: Confirm the end state on the endpoint matches what the Recommendation aimed for — persistence object gone, no longer producing the same detection.

choices.label: Wait for the SOC analyst to confirm the remediation worked.
choices.rationale: The SOC doesn't ratify the tech's verify step. The tech does the end-state check.

choices.label: Have the customer's IT contact sign off that the remediation looks fine.
choices.rationale: The customer isn't the body that ratifies remediation.

questions.explanation: Verify is about endpoint state, not report content. The portal flipping to "applied" means the command ran; verifying the state is the "did it work" check.

questions.prompt: A Critical is misrouted to your routine queue at the claim step. What's the right move?

choices.label: Claim it and start working — it's in your queue.
choices.rationale: A Critical misrouted to your queue is the moment to escalate, not to start working. Critical breaks the routine workflow.

choices: Don't claim — escalate per the runbook so the right body picks it up. The claim step is also where you decide whether the incident belongs to you.

choices.label: Claim it but flag a senior in parallel; work it while you wait.
choices.rationale: Working a Critical at the routine helpdesk level is the cardinal Critical-incidents mistake.

choices.label: Leave it unclaimed and hope someone else picks it up.
choices.rationale: Silent inaction. An active escalation is what the workflow asks for.

questions.explanation: Claiming is also a routing decision. Misrouted Criticals get surfaced, not absorbed.

questions.prompt: "The six-step workflow is overkill for routine Lows." Why is that wrong?
choices: It looks like overhead until you skip a step and create a re-detection or PSA drift. The six steps are the minimum, not the maximum.

choices.label: Lows need a senior to sign them off, so all six steps gate that approval.
choices.rationale: Lows don't need senior sign-off; that's not the reason.

choices.label: Each Low has SLA reporting requirements that demand the full six steps.
choices.rationale: SLA isn't the reason. The reason is the failure modes the steps prevent.

choices.label: Routine Lows are the only incidents where all six steps are mandatory.
choices.rationale: The six steps apply across Low and High alike.

questions.explanation: Skipping verify creates re-detections; skipping the PSA close creates drift. The minimum-not-maximum framing is the point.

questions.prompt: When should the incident note be written?
choices: While the context is fresh — during or immediately after the work.

choices.label: After-the-fact, end-of-shift, in one batch.
choices.rationale: Memory degrades; batch-writing produces vague notes that don't survive a cold read.

choices.label: Only if the senior asks you to.
choices.rationale: The note is part of the standard workflow, not on-demand.

choices.label: Just before the SOC closes the incident on their side.
choices.rationale: The SOC's close timing isn't the trigger. Fresh context is.

questions.explanation: By end-of-shift the day's incidents blur and the notes get vague.

questions.prompt: How does the six-step workflow shift between Low and High severity?
choices: Same shape; tempo, SLA clock, and pairing with other actions (isolation, user verification) differ.

choices.label: High adds two extra steps; Low has only four.
choices.rationale: The six-step spine holds across Low and High; step count doesn't change.

choices.label: High removes the verify step because the SLA clock is too tight.
choices.rationale: Tight SLA does not license skipping verify; if anything it tightens it.

choices.label: Low skips the documentation step; High keeps it.
choices.rationale: Documentation is non-optional on both.

questions.explanation: Critical breaks the shape; Low and High share it. Tempo and pairing scale with severity.

questions.prompt: Low EDR Incident Report. Host: WS-EXAMPLE-ACCOUNTING-03. Recommendation: approve the autoruns remediation. Evidence: an autoruns entry pointing to an obscure backup tool's binary. What's the right first move?

choices.label: Investigate the autoruns entry to confirm it's malicious before approving.
choices.rationale: The SOC has already triaged; re-doing the analysis is the second-guessing failure mode.

choices: Claim, review the header and Recommendation, confirm the host is in example.com, approve the autoruns remediation, verify, close in both systems, document.

choices.label: Approve the remediation, close the Huntress ticket, move on.
choices.rationale: Worst response. Skips verification, PSA close, documentation.

questions.explanation: Standard six-step workflow on a standard Low. Four to six minutes of work done cleanly.

questions.prompt: You approved a remediation; the portal shows "applied." What's the last check before you close?
choices: Confirm the persistence object no longer appears on the endpoint (re-scan in the portal or RMM remote-command).

choices.label: Skip the check; the portal said "applied," so the work is done.
choices.rationale: "Applied" means the command ran. Verifying the end state is the *did it work* check. The two are different.

choices.label: Re-read the Incident Report to make sure you understood it.
choices.rationale: Re-reading the words doesn't tell you whether the remediation worked. The verify is endpoint state.

choices.label: Ask the SOC analyst to confirm the remediation worked.
choices.rationale: The SOC doesn't ratify the verify.

questions.explanation: The Recommendation aimed for the persistence to be gone; verifying the end state is the verify step.

questions.prompt: Put the six steps of the standard EDR incident workflow in the correct order.
items: Claim the incident in the portal (and/or PSA).
Review the Incident Report — header, recommendation, affected entity.
Verify the end state on the endpoint.
Close in both Huntress and the PSA.
Document — write the incident note for the next reader.
questions.explanation: Claim, review, approve, verify, close, document. Verify and document are the most-skipped steps.

questions.prompt: Verification means re-reading the Recommendation to make sure you understood it.
questions.explanation: Verification confirms the end state on the endpoint. Re-reading the words is not the same check.
